text: The surgeon creates a tunnel beneath the lesser sac along the lesser curvature of the stomach, making an incision at the angle of His and proceeding with dissection.
The surgeon uses a stapler to create a tunnel under the posterior fundus, lifting it up before stapling it off.
The surgeon takes down the omentum to form a square pouch, preparing the anatomical structures for the gastric bypass procedure.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and creates a loop of intestine using the biliopancreatic limb, positioning it on the right side of the screen to ensure proper tension towards the gastric pouch.
The surgeon sutures the straight edge of the gastric pouch to the underside of the intestinal limb, securing the connection between these structures.

The surgeon positions the biliopancreatic limb on the right side of the screen and sews it closed. The staple line is run along the back side of the loop of bowel to maintain tension, with the eventual Roux limb positioned on the left side of the loop.
The surgeon grabs the loop of intestine, with the assistant holding it steady, and ties a surgeon's knot to secure the loop in place.
The surgeon creates a 2.5 cm anastomosis opening in the intestinal loop.
The surgeon demonstrates the anti-mesenteric position of the bowel loop, highlighting the staple line sewn to the underside of the limb.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar device as a right angle to achieve full-thickness tissue manipulation, similar to techniques used in open surgery.
The surgeon mirrors the previous incision, creating another two and a half centimeter opening. A lip is formed to facilitate sewing, preparing the tissue for further suturing.
The surgeon positions the fenestrated bipolar instrument approximately one centimeter off the staple line, using it as a right angle similar to open surgery techniques.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy, taking full thickness bites with two vicryl sutures, starting at the center and sewing around the two and a half centimeter opening.
The surgeon places the second stitch, running the suture from the left to the right and vice versa, ensuring the stitches cross over to prevent any hole at the site where they meet.
The surgeon uses a loaded needle to control the suturing process, moving swiftly to manage the tissue effectively.
The surgeon locks the corners of the incision site, ensuring secure closure as part of the hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy procedure.

The surgeon performs a series of stitching maneuvers, lifting and pushing down to set the stitch. After passing the corner, a 40 French visagee is inserted to ensure the gastric bypass loop is not narrowed.
The surgeon loads the needle and pushes down to create stitches, repeating the process on the opposite side and crossing the stitches to ensure closure at the gastrojejunostomy site.
The surgeon secures the nest muscles using four or five interrupted Lembert sutures to relieve tension, ensuring the inclusion of both the outflow and inflow areas to prevent leakage.
The surgeon performs a few Lembert sutures to relieve tension on the full thickness closure of the gastric bypass site.

The surgeon creates a slip knot by forming a C loop with the limb on the right side of the screen, using a fenestrated bipolar to tack it in place. The surgeon then switches hands to create a square knot, lifting up on the needle and pulling down on the side that exited the tissue last, before sliding the knot down to maintain tension.

The surgeon performs additional sutures, using a fenestrated instrument to tack the suture tail. The needle is lifted, and the tie that exited the tissue last is pulled down and slid to maintain tension and lock the knot.
The surgeon applies Lembert sutures to relieve tension from the muscular layer, ensuring the sutures do not overly compress the tissue.
The surgeon approaches the nearest vessel, creates a tunnel underneath it, and uses a stapler to staple off the vessel.
The surgeon traces the folds and uses a muscle sealer to take four or five bites down to the root, manipulating the tissue folds visibly.
The surgeon observes a venous congested appearance in the ciliopancreatic limb, indicating that the previous bite may have been placed too close, potentially blocking venous outflow.
The surgeon runs the Roux limb of the intestine towards the right upper quadrant, adjusting the jejunal loop or biliopancreatic limb into a candy cane configuration.
The biliopancreatic limb is positioned on the lateral side and falls into the mid-abdomen, appearing straight. The limb shows signs of venous congestion, but maintains reasonable blood flow.

The surgeon uses ICD-Green dye to assess blood flow in the biliopancreatic limb, observing a slow outflow but confirming reasonable perfusion. The limb is deemed viable, and the procedure continues without resection.
The surgeon performs two lineups and creates a one to one and a half centimeter anastomosis for the biliopancreatic limb, ensuring the passage of biliopancreatic fluid without foodstuffs.
The surgeon creates a one to one and a half centimeter opening in the elementary limb, performing an anastomosis similar to the previous one on the biliopancreatic limb.
The surgeon uses two vicryl sutures to sew around the anastomosis site, cutting each suture to a length of ten inches.
The surgeon performs the JJ's or GJ's anastomosis, which is visibly larger at twelve centimeters compared to previous anastomoses.

The surgeon performs a conversion stitch to facilitate sewing across the mesenteric defect, ensuring the stitch is not too tight. The assistant uses the assistant port to hold the left-side limb loop, preventing it from folding over and obscuring the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls the mesenteric defect towards themselves, holding it up to access both the front and back, and then sews to it, completing the Roux-en-Y configuration.
